6 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

A finance team received a video call from their CFO, asking for an urgent wire transfer ahead of a confidential acquisition. The voice was right. The face was right. The urgency felt entirely plausible given what they knew about the company’s plans. The transfer went through before anyone realized the CFO on that call had never actually joined it.
No malware was involved. No firewall was breached. No antivirus signature ever had a chance to fire, because there was nothing resembling a traditional threat for it to detect. The entire attack was generated, a synthetic voice, a synthetic face, built specifically to bypass the one thing every security system in that building was actually designed to catch.
This is the shape of the threat category traditional security was never built for, and it’s exactly what modern ai cyber security solutions are being built to address, a category growing faster than most security teams have had time to adjust to.
What Traditional Security Was Actually Built to Catch
To understand why this new category is such a problem, it helps to be specific about what came before it. Traditional security tools, firewalls, antivirus software, intrusion detection systems, all share a common foundation: they’re built to recognize known patterns.
A firewall blocks traffic matching known malicious signatures. Antivirus software flags files matching known malware fingerprints. Even more advanced systems, built around anomaly detection, are still fundamentally comparing new activity against a baseline of what’s already been seen and categorized before.
This approach works well against threats that repeat recognizable patterns, which describes the overwhelming majority of cyberattacks for the past several decades. It’s a genuinely effective model, and it’s why traditional security has kept most companies reasonably safe for so long. Ransomware variants get catalogued. Phishing templates get flagged. Malicious IP addresses get blocklisted. The entire industry has built decades of infrastructure around the assumption that threats, however numerous, eventually repeat something recognizable.
That assumption is exactly what’s now breaking down.
The Category That Doesn’t Repeat a Pattern
Generative AI has created an entirely different kind of threat, one that doesn’t rely on repeating a known pattern at all. Each attack can be freshly generated, unique, and tailored specifically to its target, which means there’s often no signature to match against in the first place.
A few examples make this concrete:

• Synthetic voice and video, used to impersonate executives convincingly enough to authorize fraudulent transactions, the way the opening scenario describes

• AI-generated phishing content, written specifically for one target using publicly available information, rather than a generic template that spam filters have learned to catch

• Prompt injection attacks, targeting a company’s own internal AI tools, manipulating them into leaking data or taking unintended actions

• Automated, adaptive malware, capable of altering its own code in real time specifically to evade whatever detection method it encounters
None of these rely on a repeated signature a traditional tool could learn to recognize. Each one is, by design, at least somewhat new every time.
How This Actually Plays Out in a Real Breach
The synthetic CFO scenario isn’t hypothetical framing. It reflects a pattern that’s played out at real companies, and it’s worth walking through how it typically unfolds.
Each phase, viewed individually, looks like normal business activity. That’s precisely what makes this category so difficult for pattern-matching systems to catch, and precisely why ai cyber security built around behavioral signals, not static rules, has become necessary rather than optional for companies handling any kind of high-value transaction or sensitive data.
What to Do When You Can’t Patch Your Way Out of This
This is the genuinely uncomfortable part for a lot of security teams. A vulnerability in software can be patched. A known malware signature can be added to a blocklist. This new category doesn’t offer that same kind of fix, because the threat isn’t a static flaw waiting to be closed.
What actually helps here is a shift in approach rather than a single new tool. A few things matter more now than they did before:

• Behavioral verification protocols for high-stakes requests, a callback to a known number, a pre-agreed verification phrase, something that doesn’t rely on trusting what’s seen or heard in the moment

• Employee awareness specifically about synthetic media, since most security training still focuses on obviously suspicious emails, not a convincingly real-looking video call

• AI-native detection systems designed to analyze behavioral patterns and content authenticity, rather than matching against known signatures, since this new threat category has no consistent signature to match

• Slower authorization processes for high-risk actions, deliberately introducing friction into large transfers or sensitive requests, even when the request appears urgent and legitimate
None of these fully close the gap on their own. Together, they shift the defense from “can we recognize this specific attack” toward “can we make any single attack, however convincing, harder to act on immediately.”
Why This Requires AI-Native Defense, Not Just More of the Old Tools
It’s tempting to respond to this new category by simply adding more traditional tools, another firewall rule, another detection signature. This tends to produce diminishing returns, since the fundamental limitation isn’t coverage, it’s the underlying approach.
Genuinely effective ai cyber security solutions work differently. Rather than matching against known patterns, they’re built to detect behavioral anomalies and content authenticity issues in real time, the kind of subtle inconsistencies a synthetic voice or an AI-generated message might carry that a human, or a signature-based tool, would miss entirely.
This distinction matters because ai cyber security built around behavioral analysis can, at least in principle, catch a genuinely novel attack it’s never specifically seen before, precisely because it’s not looking for a repeat of something already catalogued. Companies evaluating ai cyber security solutions for the first time should specifically ask whether the system relies on pattern matching or behavioral analysis, since that single distinction predicts how well it will handle this new category of threat.
One Detail Worth Knowing
Rubixe’s approach to this category specifically includes running simulated synthetic media attacks against a client’s own team, testing whether staff can actually spot a convincing deepfake call under realistic pressure, rather than relying purely on technical detection systems to catch everything before it reaches a human.
Frequently Asked Questions
Q: How common are synthetic media attacks like the one described in the opening scenario? They’re still less common than traditional phishing, but growing quickly as the tools to generate convincing synthetic voice and video become cheaper and more accessible, lowering the barrier for attackers considerably.
Q: Can traditional antivirus and firewall tools be updated to catch this new category?
Not effectively on their own. These tools are fundamentally built around pattern matching, which struggles against threats specifically designed to avoid repeating any recognizable pattern.
Q: Is AI security only relevant for large enterprises, or should smaller companies worry about this too?
Smaller companies are increasingly targeted too, partly because they often have fewer verification protocols in place, making them easier targets even with a less sophisticated attack. AI security isn’t a large-enterprise problem exclusively; it scales down to any business handling sensitive transactions or data.
Q: What’s the single most effective defense against this category of threat?
A behavioral verification protocol for high-stakes requests, something that doesn’t rely on trusting what’s seen or heard in the moment, tends to be more reliably effective than any single detection tool.
The finance team that authorized that wire transfer weren’t careless. They followed a request that looked, sounded, and felt completely legitimate, because it was built specifically to feel that way. No amount of general caution or standard security awareness training would have caught it, because nothing about the interaction matched what that training was designed to flag.
That’s the actual shift security teams need to internalize. The threat isn’t getting past defenses by being sneakier within familiar patterns. It’s succeeding by not fitting any familiar pattern at all, which means the defense itself has to change, not just get more of the same. The companies handling this well aren’t the ones with the most security tools installed. They’re the ones who’ve accepted that the old model of catching known threats needs a genuinely different companion approach, one built for threats that are, by design, never quite the same twice.
